Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is the part of analytics I care about most—turning data into a story that actually changes a decision. At NextGen I analyzed 2,000+ data points, found the top driver of engagement, and built a report the C-suite kept referencing long after I left. I'd love to do that for recruiting specifically, where the questions—where are we losing candidates, what's driving time-to-fill—are exactly the kind I enjoy digging into. Being a strategic data partner to a recruiting org, not just a report-builder, is the work I want.
Why do you want to work at Discord?: Honestly, I admire how Discord has built genuine community at scale, and I'd be glad to help the team that brings in the people building it. The role also fits where I want to grow: I'm strongest at the analytical thinking and storytelling side of people data, and I want to deepen the technical tooling around it. Discord pairing recruiting analytics with real data infrastructure is exactly the environment to do that. I'd bring strong research design, recruiting-domain knowledge, and a track record of influencing decisions with data.
How would you describe your data analysi: I'll be candid and specific. My analysis has lived in advanced Excel, Google Sheets, and SPSS—I'm comfortable with mixed-methods work, building reports and visualizations, and structuring messy data into something decision-grade. At NextGen and in my ODN consulting I ran analyses that influenced executive decisions and quantified $362K in costs. Where I'm honest about a gap is SQL, Tableau, Snowflake, and One Model—I haven't worked in those yet. But I learn tooling fast: I use AI tools daily and have been building a RAG-based data system in my own time, so I'm not intimidated by a new technical stack. The analytical judgment and recruiting context transfer directly while I ramp on the tools.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC, where research design and measurement were core to my training. I diagnose with data, partner with stakeholders on what to measure, and tell the story that drives the decision. I also handle confidential people data with care—discretion is second nature in HR work. I'm upfront that I'd be building SQL and BI-tool fluency on the job, and confident in the analytical and storytelling strengths I'd bring on day one.
